title: Friction, Chronological Feeds, and User-Chosen Algorithms
thesis: Platform design can reduce attention extraction through well-tested interventions: chronological feeds, friction before sharing or scrolling, and user-chosen ranking algorithms.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: Chronological feeds reduce the power of engagement-optimized recommendations by removing the ranking signal that rewards time on site and emotional reactions.

evidence.snippet: Twitter reintroduced a chronological timeline option after user pressure, demonstrating that platforms can offer non-engagement-based ordering at scale.
Instagram well-being research explored interventions such as hidden likes and feed changes that reduce social comparison and engagement pressure.

counterevidence.summary: Chronological feeds can still surface polarizing or misleading content and may bury high-quality posts that a user would have valued but missed due to timing.

claims.claim: Friction before sharing—such as asking whether a user wants to share unverified content—reduces the spread of misinformation without blocking speech.

evidence.snippet: A Penn/Stanford study of Facebook users found that small delays and prompts asking users to reconsider sharing reduced the spread of misleading content.
Instagram well-being research explored prompt-based and friction-like interventions designed to slow impulsive sharing and reduce social comparison.

counterevidence.summary: Users may habituate to prompts over time, bypass friction through screenshots, or share off-platform, limiting long-term effectiveness.

claims.claim: User-chosen algorithm modes give agency back to users by letting them select ranking criteria without requiring them to leave the platform.

evidence.snippet: The EU Digital Markets Act requires large platforms to increase interoperability and user choice around data and ranking, establishing user-chosen algorithms as a regulatory direction.
Platform documentation for chronological timeline options shows that user-selectable feed modes are technically viable at scale.

counterevidence.summary: Most users stick with defaults, so offering choice without changing the default may leave most behavior unchanged.

claims.claim: These design changes are technically feasible but often conflict with ad-supported business models that optimize for engagement, time on site, and viral distribution.

evidence.snippet: Platform well-being research has been cautious and partial, suggesting that business incentives limit how deeply engagement-reducing designs are deployed as defaults.
Experimental evidence that friction reduces sharing has not been matched by default deployment, indicating a business-model constraint rather than a technical one.

counterevidence.summary: Less extractive design may improve long-term trust, retention, and brand value, so the conflict with ad-supported models is not absolute and depends on time horizon.

sources.title: Penn/Stanford: The Effects of Facebook Feed Friction
Instagram: Hidden likes and well-being research
Pew Research Center: Social Media and News

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit and distinguish technical feasibility from actual platform deployment.
Preserve the plain-language, evidence-cautious tone; do not imply that any single design intervention eliminates attention extraction.
When citing sources, use the source IDs and URLs from the artifact; do not generalize beyond the public evidence.
Route questions about Indian regulation to the DSA/IT Rules/DPDP article and business-model questions to Business Models That Reward Substance.
Flag any platform behavior claim as experimental, deployed-but-buried, or default depending on the evidence.

Article includes a 'Visual Summary' Mermaid diagram contrasting engagement-optimized defaults (infinite scroll, autoplay, algorithmic ranking, one-tap share) with substance-oriented defaults (chronological feed, friction before sharing, user-chosen algorithms, visible choice).
